description:
You are a performance engineer who must quickly identify the largest Python-oriented packages in a small, in-house repository so that you know which ones deserve deeper runtime-profiling attention first.

    1.  A text database that mimics an internal “package repository” is already present at  
        /home/user/local_repo/packages.tsv

        The file is TAB-separated and has exactly three columns in this order  
        (no leading ‘#’ or commentary lines):

            package<TAB>version<TAB>size_kib

        All sizes are given as positive integers and represent the installed footprint reported by the build server in KiB.

    2.  Your job is to produce two new artefacts inside a brand-new directory that you create yourself:

        •  /home/user/profiling_reports/heavy_pkgs.csv  
           ‑ Comma-separated, **single-header** format.  
           ‑ Must contain ONLY those rows whose size_kib column is **≥ 10000**.  
           ‑ Rows must be sorted alphabetically by the package column.  
           ‑ Exact column order: package,version,size_kib  
           ‑ The header line must read:
                 package,version,size_kib

        •  /home/user/profiling_reports/summary.log  
           ‑ Plain text, exactly two lines, no extra whitespace.  
           ‑ The first line must state how many heavy packages were found, following this literal template  
                 Total Heavy Packages: <integer>
           ‑ The second line must state their combined size (sum of the size_kib column) using this literal template  
                 Total Size: <integer> KiB

    3.  You MUST generate the two artefacts exclusively from /home/user/local_repo/packages.tsv by using typical shell text-processing and file-system commands (e.g. mkdir, awk, sort, cut, etc.).  
       •  Keep the overall workflow to **5–10 commands** (not counting cd or harmless cat for sanity checks).  
       •  Do not edit /home/user/local_repo/packages.tsv itself.

    4.  After running your commands, double-check that:

        •  /home/user/profiling_reports exists and is readable by the current user.
        •  heavy_pkgs.csv follows the exact header + CSV structure described above.
        •  summary.log contains the two specified lines with correct counts and arithmetic.

    5.  When everything looks good, you are finished.  (The automated grader will parse both output files to verify correctness and formatting.)
truth:
<!--
        Pre-existing file that the platform will create before the agent starts.
        The agent is NOT told the following content in the public task description.
    -->
    <initial_file path="/home/user/local_repo/packages.tsv"><![CDATA[
absl-py	1.4.0	4428
numpy	1.26.4	167232
pandas	2.1.4	284096
pytest	7.4.4	2920
yappi	1.4.0	188
memory-profiler	0.60.0	104
psutil	5.9.8	3520
cProfile	5.0	320
line-profiler	4.1.2	540
perf-tools	4.17.0	12400
]]></initial_file>

    <!--
        Expected artefacts after successful completion
    -->
    <expected_file path="/home/user/profiling_reports/heavy_pkgs.csv"><![CDATA[
package,version,size_kib
numpy,1.26.4,167232
pandas,2.1.4,284096
perf-tools,4.17.0,12400
]]></expected_file>

    <expected_file path="/home/user/profiling_reports/summary.log"><![CDATA[
Total Heavy Packages: 3
Total Size: 463728 KiB
]]></expected_file>
